extra.message:
Missing Rate Limiting on Student Creation Endpoint

The POST /students/ endpoint for creating students has no rate limiting applied.

Impact: Attackers can flood the endpoint with requests, creating large numbers of student records and potentially causing denial of service or database exhaustion.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on state-changing POST endpoints.

Stored XSS via user fields (first_name, last_name, username) in Templates

User fields including `first_name`, `middle_name`, `last_name`, and `username` are stored in the database and rendered in templates without escaping (due to global autoescape=False). If any of these fields contain malicious HTML/JavaScript, they will execute in users' browsers.

Impact: Stored XSS: malicious scripts injected into user profile fields will execute for all users who view pages rendering those fields (e.g., navigation bar showing logged-in user's name).

Fix: Enable autoescape globally in Jinja2 configuration, or explicitly escape all user field template expressions using the `|e` filter.

Jinja2 Autoescape Disabled Globally

The Jinja2 template engine is configured with `autoescape=False`, which disables HTML escaping for all template variables across the entire application. Any user-controlled data rendered in templates will be output as raw HTML, enabling stored or reflected XSS attacks.

Impact: Attackers can inject malicious JavaScript into pages viewed by other users, leading to session hijacking, credential theft, defacement, or other client-side attacks.

Fix: Enable autoescape by setting `autoescape=True` or using `autoescape=select_autoescape(['html', 'xml'])`. Explicitly mark trusted content as safe using `|safe` filter only where necessary.

Stored XSS via course.title in Course Template

The `course.title` field is stored in the database and rendered in templates without escaping (due to global autoescape=False). An attacker who can create or modify a course title can inject malicious HTML/JavaScript that will execute in the browsers of all users viewing the course.

Impact: Stored XSS: malicious scripts injected into course titles will execute for all users viewing course listings or detail pages.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally in Jinja2 configuration, or explicitly escape `{{ course.title }}` in all templates using the `|e` filter.

Stored XSS via review.review_text in Review Template

The `review.review_text` field is stored in the database and rendered in templates without escaping (due to global autoescape=False). An attacker who can submit a course review with malicious content can inject HTML/JavaScript that executes for all users viewing the course review page.

Impact: Stored XSS: malicious scripts injected into review text will execute for all users viewing course review pages.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally in Jinja2 configuration, or explicitly escape `{{ review.review_text }}` in all templates using the `|e` filter.

SQL Injection via String Formatting in Student.create()

The `Student.create()` method constructs a SQL query using Python's `%` string formatting operator directly with user-supplied `name` input. The query is built as `"INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES ('%(name)s')" % {'name': name}`, which performs Python string interpolation before the query is passed to the database driver. This means the `name` value is embedded directly into the SQL string without parameterization, allowing an attacker to inject arbitrary SQL.

Impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary SQL commands, potentially reading, modifying, or deleting any data in the database, or escalating to OS-level commands depending on database configuration.

Fix: Use parameterized queries. Replace the string formatting with: `q = "INSERT INTO students (name) VALUES (%s)"` and call `cur.execute(q, (name,))`.

Stored XSS via student.name in Student Template

The `student.name` field is stored in the database and rendered in templates without escaping (due to global autoescape=False). An attacker who can create a student with a malicious name can inject HTML/JavaScript that executes for all users viewing student listings or detail pages.

Impact: Stored XSS: malicious scripts injected into student names will execute for all users viewing student listings or detail pages.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally in Jinja2 configuration, or explicitly escape `{{ student.name }}` in all templates using the `|e` filter.

CSRF Middleware Commented Out

The CSRF protection middleware (`csrf_middleware`) is commented out in the application setup. While the middleware implementation exists in `middlewares.py`, it is not active, leaving all POST endpoints unprotected against Cross-Site Request Forgery attacks.

Impact: Attackers can craft malicious pages that submit forged POST requests on behalf of authenticated users, potentially creating students, courses, marks, or reviews without the user's knowledge.

Fix: Uncomment `csrf_middleware` in the middleware list to re-enable CSRF protection for all POST requests.

Weak Password Hashing Using MD5

User passwords are hashed using MD5 (`hashlib.md5`), which is a cryptographically broken hash function. MD5 is extremely fast to compute, making it trivial to brute-force or crack using rainbow tables. It provides no salt, making all users with the same password have the same hash.

Impact: If the database is compromised, all user passwords can be rapidly cracked using precomputed rainbow tables or GPU-accelerated brute force attacks.

Fix: Replace MD5 with a proper password hashing algorithm such as bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 (e.g., using the `passlib` library). Always use a unique salt per password.

XSS via Error Object Rendered in 40x Error Template

The error middleware passes the raw exception object `exc` to the `errors/40x.jinja2` template as `{'error': exc}`. With autoescape disabled, any template expression rendering `error` or its attributes (such as `{{ error }}`, `{{ error.text }}`, `{{ error.reason }}`) will output unescaped content. If the exception message contains user-controlled data (e.g., from URL parameters or request data), this could lead to reflected XSS.

Impact: Reflected XSS if error messages contain user-controlled data and are rendered unescaped in the error template.

Fix: Enable autoescape globally, or ensure error templates escape all rendered exception data. Avoid rendering raw exception objects in templates.

Missing Rate Limiting on Login Endpoint

The index route (GET/POST /) which handles login has no rate limiting applied. An attacker can make unlimited login attempts to brute-force user credentials.

Impact: Attackers can perform unlimited brute-force or credential stuffing attacks against the login endpoint without any throttling or lockout.

Fix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint, e.g., using aiohttp-limiter or a Redis-based rate limiter, to restrict the number of login attempts per IP or username.

Redis Connection Without Authentication (Service Layer)

The Redis connection pool is created without any authentication credentials. No password is passed to `aioredis.create_pool()`.

Impact: Unauthenticated access to Redis allows attackers to read or forge session tokens, potentially compromising all active user sessions.

Fix: Pass a `password` parameter to `aioredis.create_pool()` using a value from the application configuration.

Full User Object (Including Password Hash) Passed to Template Context

Based on the import `from sqli.utils.jinja2 import csrf_processor, auth_user_processor` in app.py, the `auth_user_processor` context processor injects user data into every template context. The `User` NamedTuple (defined in dao/user.py) includes the `pwd_hash` field. If the full User object is passed to templates, the password hash is available in every template rendering context, crossing a trust boundary.

Impact: The MD5 password hash of the authenticated user is exposed in the template context on every page render. Combined with disabled autoescape, this could be rendered to users or logged.

Fix: In `auth_user_processor`, pass only safe fields (e.g., `id`, `username`, `first_name`, `is_admin`) to the template context rather than the full User object containing `pwd_hash`.

Debug Mode Enabled in Production Application

The aiohttp Application is initialized with `debug=True`. This enables verbose error reporting and may expose internal stack traces, configuration details, and other sensitive information to end users.

Impact: Detailed error messages and stack traces may be exposed to attackers, revealing internal application structure, file paths, and configuration details that aid further exploitation.

Fix: Set `debug=False` in production, or make it configurable via environment variable or config file.

SQL Injection Risk via Incorrect Parameterization in Course.get_many() and Student.get_many()

The `get_many()` methods in both `Course` and `Student` DAOs pass `**params` (dict unpacking) as keyword arguments to `cur.execute()`, but the aiopg/psycopg2 `execute()` method expects the parameters as a positional argument (a tuple or dict), not as keyword arguments. This means the parameterized values are never actually bound to the query, and the `%(limit)s` / `%(offset)s` placeholders may be left unsubstituted or cause errors. Additionally, the query strings contain `LIMIT + %(limit)s` with a stray `+` operator which is unusual. While this may not be directly exploitable for injection (since limit/offset are validated as integers), the incorrect API usage is a security concern.

Impact: Incorrect parameterization could lead to query errors or unexpected behavior. If the parameters are not properly bound, the query may fail or behave unexpectedly.

Fix: Pass params as a positional argument: `await cur.execute(q, params)` where params is a dict. Also remove the stray `+` from `LIMIT + %(limit)s`.

Session Cookie Missing HttpOnly Flag

The Redis session storage is configured with `httponly=False`, which means the session cookie is accessible to JavaScript running in the browser. Combined with the XSS vulnerabilities present due to disabled autoescape, this makes session hijacking trivial.

Impact: If an XSS vulnerability is exploited, attackers can read the session cookie via `document.cookie` and hijack authenticated user sessions.

Fix: Set `httponly=True` on the session storage to prevent JavaScript access to the session cookie.

Redis Configuration Missing Password Field

The Redis configuration schema defines only `host`, `port`, and `db` fields, with no `password` field. This means there is no mechanism to configure Redis authentication, and the application will connect to Redis without a password.

Impact: If Redis is accessible on the network without authentication, attackers can read or manipulate session data, potentially hijacking user sessions or injecting malicious session content.

Fix: Add a `password` field to the Redis config schema and pass it to `aioredis.create_pool()` in `services/redis.py`.

Database Connection String Without TLS

The PostgreSQL DSN is constructed without any SSL/TLS parameters (`sslmode=require` or similar). Database traffic including credentials and query results may be transmitted in cleartext.

Impact: Database credentials and sensitive data (student records, marks, user passwords) may be intercepted by network attackers via man-in-the-middle attacks.

Fix: Add `sslmode=require` (or `sslmode=verify-full`) to the DSN to enforce encrypted connections to the database.
